title: for Loops
subtitle: Iterate over sequences one item at a time
learning_objectives: Write a for loop that iterates over a string or list
Use enumerate() to access both the index and value
Explain the difference between for and while loops

sections.heading: for Loop Syntax: Iterating Over a Sequence
sections.content:
A for loop visits each item in a sequence one at a time. You choose a variable name (commonly item, char, or num), and Python assigns each element to it in turn. Unlike a while loop, you don't need to manage a counter — Python handles it automatically.

You can iterate over any iterable: strings, lists, tuples, and more.
sections.code_example:
# Iterating over a string
for char in "hello":
    print(char)

print("---")

# Iterating over a list
for item in [10, 20, 30]:
    print(item)
sections.warning: Infinite loops will freeze your browser! Always make sure your loop condition will eventually become False.

sections.heading: Iterating Over Lists
sections.content: Lists are the most common thing to iterate with a for loop. Each element is visited once, in order. You'll learn much more about lists in Module 4 — for now, just notice how naturally for handles them.
sections.code_example:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

for fruit in fruits:
    print("I like", fruit)

print("Total fruits:", len(fruits))
sections.code_output:
I like apple
I like banana
I like cherry
Total fruits: 3

sections.heading: enumerate(): Index and Value Together
sections.content:
enumerate() wraps an iterable and yields both the index (position, starting at 0) and the value at each step. Use it when you need to know where you are in the sequence as well as what the current item is.

The syntax unpacks two values: for i, value in enumerate(iterable):

exercises.title: Character Printer
exercises.prompt: Use a for loop to print each character of the string "code" on its own line.

exercises.title: Shopping List
exercises.prompt: Given items = ["milk", "eggs", "bread"], use a for loop to print each item prefixed with "- ". For example: - milk Note: print("-", item) adds a space, giving '- milk'. That is the expected output here.
exercises.starter_code:
items = ["milk", "eggs", "bread"]

# Write your for loop below
hints: print("-", item) will print a dash followed by the item

exercises.title: Numbered List with enumerate
exercises.prompt:
Use enumerate() to print each item in ["red", "green", "blue"] with its position starting at 1. Output should look like:
1. red
2. green
3. blue Note: This exercise uses str() and + to format the output (e.g. str(i) + ". " + color). These are covered in Module 2 — the hint will show you exactly how to write it if you need it.
hints: Use: for i, color in enumerate(colors, start=1):
You can combine the index and color with: str(i) + ". " + color

quiz.question: What does the for loop variable (e.g. 'char' in 'for char in word') represent?
options: The current item from the sequence on each iteration
The index position of the current item
A copy of the entire sequence
quiz.explanation: On each iteration, Python assigns the next item from the sequence to the loop variable. So 'char' holds one character at a time when iterating over a string.

quiz.question: What does enumerate() add to a regular for loop?
options: It makes the loop run faster
It provides both the index and the value on each iteration
It converts every item to a string
quiz.explanation: enumerate() wraps an iterable and yields a (index, value) pair on each iteration. This lets you access both the position and the item without maintaining a separate counter variable.

completion_message: Great job! for loops are one of the most-used features in Python. You can iterate over strings, lists, and almost anything else. Next, you'll learn about range() — the perfect companion for for loops.
